claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child or children.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility rules differ based on age:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, or if you have multiple children, let me know and we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me work through Alex's eligibility.

Since Alex is 8 years old, they fall into the "under 16" category. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good news - since Alex is only 8 years old, they wouldn't be working full-time, so we can move forward. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This would include benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that are claimed in Alex's own name (not yours). For an 8-year-old, this is quite unusual, so likely the answer is no - but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, they wouldn't be in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this to continue with the assessment. Apprenticeships are typically for young people aged 16+.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're far too young for that!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You're absolutely right! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to check your residency status:**

To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must meet UK residency requirements. You can qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** - this is the most common situation
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, or have sufficient resources)
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK?** 

Or if not, do any of the other circumstances apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible for Alex if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex** - this is the most common situation
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex?** Or if not, do you contribute to their upkeep in one of these ways?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another guardian or family member
- Anyone else who might have already made a claim

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to agree to stop their claim and transfer it to you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Where is Alex currently living?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply:

- Is Alex in hospital?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex in a foster care arrangement?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you?

Which of these situations applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives at home with me normally, but they have been in local authority care for a couple of months.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has been in local authority care, I need to follow that pathway as it has specific rules about eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about Alex's time in care:**

You mentioned Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months. The rules are:

- Child Benefit can continue for up to **8 weeks** if a child is in care
- **OR** you can still claim if Alex spends at least **24 hours per week** at home with you

So I need to know:

1. **Has Alex been in care for 8 weeks or less?** (You mentioned "a couple of months" which might be over 8 weeks)
2. **Does Alex spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you?** (For example, regular home visits or staying with you on weekends)

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has been in care for exactly 8 weeks, so it's right at the limit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that clarification. Since Alex has been in care for exactly 8 weeks (which is at the limit but not over), you still meet this requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

Since Alex is in local authority care, I need to know if the council is financially supporting Alex's accommodation or care costs. 

Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's care.

**Is the council paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit

**For Alex (age 8):** ✅ **ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements:
- Alex is under 16 years old
- Alex is not working full-time or receiving benefits in their own right
- You live in the UK and meet residency requirements
- You live with Alex and are responsible for them
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Although Alex has been in local authority care, it's been exactly 8 weeks (within the allowed limit)
- The council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance

---

### **Next Steps - How to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate

### **Important Things to Know:**

⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the tax system. However, you can still claim it to get National Insurance credits (which protect your State Pension), or you can opt not to receive the payments.

💷 **Payment Schedule:** Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits).

### **Useful Links:**
- How to claim: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim
- General eligibility information: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old (under 16), (2) Alex is not working full-time, (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, meeting residency requirements, (6) You live with Alex and are responsible for them, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex has been in local authority care for exactly 8 weeks (within the 8-week limit), and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old child Alex. Despite Alex being in local authority care, the claim is valid because it's been exactly 8 weeks (at the limit) and the council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
